coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in carbon dots as powerful ecofriendly corrosion inhibitors for copper and its alloy
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Jianbin
Chu, Qingkai
Zhao, Songmei
Liu, Zhixiong
Zhang, Xianhui
coredata.dc:description: Copper, as the fifth most usual metal in the earth's crust, is very useful in pure or alloying form. The metallic systems constructed from copper is susceptible to corrosion in the aggressive medium like the acidic solution. Many efficient strategies for protecting copper from corrosion have been developed. Among them, using corrosion inhibitors is considered be a most popular technique. Many commercial inorganic and organic compounds are effective inhibitors, but most of them are toxic and the synthetic procedure is environmentally threatening. Because of their ecofriendly and good water solubility, recently carbon dots (CDs) are also being used as green aqueous phase corrosion inhibitors. In this review, research progress on CDs, especially those heteroatom-doped CDs as corrosion inhibitors for copper and its alloy is systematically summarized. The synthetic procedure, the morphology and the structure of the various CDs are discussed. The corrosion resistance performance, kinetics, thermodynamics, quantum computational chemistry and inhibition mechanism of CDs for copper metal are fully reviewed. Moreover, challenges, suggestions and opportunities regarding the development of CDs as corrosion inhibitors for copper are presented. The knowledge and inspiration gained from this review will deepen the current understanding of CDs as corrosion inhibitors and motivate more scientists to provide valuable contributions to this topic in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Corrosion inhibitor
Copper
Ecofriendly

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Classification of CDs as corrosion inhibitors for copper and its alloy
N, S-co-doped CDs as corrosion inhibitors
Metal ion-co-doped CDs as corrosion inhibitors

Project of Subsidy Funds for Marine Economic Development in Fujian Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research has been encouraged and supported by Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province ( 202303021211329 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province ( 2021J01847 ), the Project of Subsidy Funds for Marine Economic Development in Fujian Province ( FJHJF-L-2022-20 ) and the Open Fund of Fujian Province Key Laboratory of Ship and Ocean Engineering . Dr. Zhao would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22103092 ) for the financial supporting.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, (b) AFM images and High-resolution X-ray photoelectron deconvoluted profiles of (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) O 1s of NCDs. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [42]. Copyright (2019), Elsevier.

(a) Nyquist diagram, (b) Bode graph for the copper samples immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing different concentrations of NCDs, equivalent circuits used to fit the EIS data in (c) blank solution and (d) inhibited acid solution with the NCDs. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [42]. Copyright (2019), Elsevier.

(a) Polarization curves for the Cu measured in 0.5 M H2SO4 with different concentrations of NCDs; (b) Equivalent circuits for fitting the EIS, Nyquist diagram, Bode diagram of copper electrode recorded in the corrosive medium at 298 K with different concentrations; (c) The adsorption and anti-corrosion mechanism of NCDs on the copper in acid medium is proposed in the diagram. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [43]. Copyright (2021), Elsevier.
Compendium diagram of the synthetic route (a) andTEM image (b) of N-CDs. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [44]. Copyright (2021), Elsevier.

SEM images (a, b) and contact angle appearance (c, d) of the copper after 24 h immersed in 3 wt% NaCl solution without and with 200 mg/L NCDs. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [45]. Copyright (2022), Elsevier.

(a) Schematic illustration for the synthesis of N, S-CDs (the left is N, S-CDs dissolved in ethanol and the right in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution); (B) Polarization curve of copper in 0.5 M H₂SO₄ solution with different concentrations of N, S-CDs at 298 K; XPS information for (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s, (f) S 2p of corresponding N, S-CDs.; Reprinted with permission from Ref. [48]. Copyright (2021), Elsevier.

Potential difference distribution of Cu after different immersion time in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without and with 70 ppm N, S-CDs inhibitor: 1 h (a1, a2), 6 h (b1, b2), 12 h (c1, c2) and 24 h (d1, d2). Reprinted with permission from Ref. [49]. Copyright (2021), Elsevier.

Typical force-distance curves (a), corresponding forces (b), SEM micromorphology measured on the Cu samples after 24 h immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution in the absence (c) and presence (d) of 70 ppm N, S-CDs inhibitor. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [49]. Copyright (2021), Elsevier.
The optimized geometry (a), LUMO (b), HOMO (c), ESP map (d) of N, S-CDs. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [50]. Copyright (2023), Elsevier.

(a) Three-dimensional morphology and surface roughness of copper after immersion and (b) Two-dimensional morphology and EDS analysis of copper surface after corrosion. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [52]. Copyright (2022), Elsevier.

Characterization of the structure and functional groups of BCDs: (a) ART-FTIR, (b) UV–vis, (c) XRD, (d) TEM, and (e) Schematic diagram of the structural composition of BCDs. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [57]. Copyright (2023), Elsevier.

Curves of open circuit potential versus time (a) copper soaked in different concentrations of BCDs H2SO4 solution at 298 K, (b) copper soaked in 50 mg/L BCDs H2SO4 solution at different temperatures, (c) copper soaked in blank solution at different temperatures, the curve of the PDP. (d) The copper soaked in different concentrations of BCDs H2SO4 solution at 298 K, (e) copper soaked in 50 mg/L BCDs H2SO4 solution at various temperatures, (f) copper soaked in blank solution at different temperatures. (g) Linear fitting of Arrhenius equation of copper. (h) Linear fitting of transition state equation. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [57]. Copyright (2023), Elsevier.

(a) The schematic diagram of preparation process of WEP FCDs/BNNS coating; The LEIS diagrams of WEP (a), WEP BNNS (b), WEP FCDs (c) and WEP FCDs/BNNS (d) coatings after 40 d immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [61]. Copyright (2022), Elsevier.

The corrosion protection mechanism of WEP (a), WEP BNNS (b), WEP FCDs (c) and WEP FCDs/BNNS (d) coatings immersing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution Reprinted with permission from Ref. [61]. Copyright (2022), Elsevier.
A summary of corrosion inhibition potential of CDs for copper and its alloy.

head.ce:title: Recent advances in carbon dots as powerful ecofriendly corrosion inhibitors for copper and its alloy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Marine Engineering College, Jimei University, Xiamen, Fujian, 361021, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Marine Engineering College, Jimei University, Xiamen, Fujian 361021, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Materials and Advanced Manufacturing, Beijing Academy of Science and Technology, Beijing, 100089, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of New Materials and Advanced Manufacturing
Beijing Academy of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of New Materials and Advanced Manufacturing, Beijing Academy of Science and Technology, Beijing, 100089, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong, 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper, as the fifth most usual metal in the earth's crust, is very useful in pure or alloying form. The metallic systems constructed from copper is susceptible to corrosion in the aggressive medium like the acidic solution. Many efficient strategies for protecting copper from corrosion have been developed. Among them, using corrosion inhibitors is considered be a most popular technique. Many commercial inorganic and organic compounds are effective inhibitors, but most of them are toxic and the synthetic procedure is environmentally threatening. Because of their ecofriendly and good water solubility, recently carbon dots (CDs) are also being used as green aqueous phase corrosion inhibitors. In this review, research progress on CDs, especially those heteroatom-doped CDs as corrosion inhibitors for copper and its alloy is systematically summarized. The synthetic procedure, the morphology and the structure of the various CDs are discussed. The corrosion resistance performance, kinetics, thermodynamics, quantum computational chemistry and inhibition mechanism of CDs for copper metal are fully reviewed. Moreover, challenges, suggestions and opportunities regarding the development of CDs as corrosion inhibitors for copper are presented. The knowledge and inspiration gained from this review will deepen the current understanding of CDs as corrosion inhibitors and motivate more scientists to provide valuable contributions to this topic in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper and its alloys are the most widely used metals having a wide range of applications in industries including electronic industries, marine industries, power stations, heat exchangers and cooling towers due to the favorable properties like mechanical workability and malleability, good thermal and electrical conductivity [1–3]. Generally, the copper could show appropriate corrosion resistance in the air because of the formation of a protective film from the corrosion products on its surface [4,5]. However, the metallic systems constructed from copper is susceptible to corrosion in the presence of oxygen and some aggressive acidic medium such as hydrochloric acid and sulfuric acid [6,7]. In the gas and oil industry, acidification is widely used to remove the corrosion deposits and scale inside the copper pipes. During the acid fracturing process, the acid comes into contact with copper metal, and its dissolution occurs. The corrosion of copper and formation of corrosion products on its surface not only has a negative effect on the mechanical, physical or esthetical properties but also may cause a huge economic loss and ecological disaster [8–10]. Therefore, the corrosion protection of copper metal is of great importance and a challenging issue.

A variety of efficient strategies to protect copper from corrosion have been developed [11–13]. Among them, using corrosion inhibitors is considered a popular technique to prevent or mitigate the corrosion rate of metallic copper due to the advantages of simple operation, strong adaptability and less damage to the original systems constructed from copper [9,14–16]. These compounds can effectively slow down the copper corrosion and inhibit, delay, or even completely prevent anodic or cathodic reactions when added a small concentration into the corrosive media [17,18]. A wide variety of corrosion inhibitors have been prepared and the corrosion-resistance behavior was further evaluated [9,14]. The corrosion inhibitors used for copper can be simply classified into inorganic and organic corrosion inhibitors. Inorganic corrosion inhibitor including silicates, nitrates, nitrites, molybates, phosphates, and borates could oxidize the copper to form an impervious layer to prevent the direct ion-metal interaction and thus retards the rate of copper dissolution in the aggressive medium [19,20]. However, most of these inorganic substances are toxic and provide weak corrosion inhibition efficiency. Compared with inorganic corrosion inhibitors, the organic corrosion inhibitors showed a high corrosion inhibition efficiency (IE) because the organic compounds could be firmly adsorbed onto the copper surface to form a protective film by a physical and/or chemical adsorption and thus effectively shield the copper surface from the aggressive species attack [21,22]. The structure of these organic compounds plays a key role in determining the binding strength between the organic substance and the copper's surface. In this case, the presence of electron-rich heteroatoms including nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus and sulfur as effective active sites could promote the electron exchange between corrosion inhibitor and surface atoms and thus resulted in a strongly adhesive protective layer on the copper surface [23]. Therefore, the organic corrosion inhibitor usually contains heteroatoms and/or polar functional groups such as –OH, –COOC2H5, –COOH, -C–O, –NH2, unsaturated covalent bonds and delocalized π electrons, etc. A large number of organic compounds such as azole compounds (e.g. thiazole, benzotriazole, imidazole and thiadiazole), amines, Schiff bases, amino acids and pharmaceutical compounds were investigated as corrosion inhibitors used for protection of copper [9]. However, most of these inhibitors are also toxic and the synthetic procedure is environmentally threatening, which greatly limits their continuous use in the industry.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm [24–26]. CDs, as a new rising star in the carbon family, has attracts intensive interests due to their various industrial and biological applications [27–30] in biomedicine, bioimaging, sensors, optoelectronics and photocatalysis. CDs have some unique advantages including extremely high and harmonize photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, substantial biocompatibility, inertness towards chemicals, magnified adsorption ability, cost-effectivity, eco-friendliness, high solubility, excellent stability and etc. Various protocols including hydrothermal reactions, ultrasonication microwave irradiation, electrochemical oxidation and laser ablation have been developed to prepare CDs [31]. Ascorbic acid, citric acid, food wastes, ammonium citrate and amino acid and other natural materials were applied for the synthesis of CDs [32,33]. The precursors are commonly abundant in natural sources, low-cost, and renewable and the synthetic procedure are environmental environmentally friendly. Meanwhile, the surface chemistry of CDs could be suitably modified by doping them with S, N, O and P heteroatoms and thus result in tunable physiochemical properties [34–36]. Similar to traditional heterocyclic corrosion inhibitors, CDs could also be adsorbed onto the copper surface to form a protective layer to prevent the corrosion process occurring. In addition, the higher coverage of the inhibitors on metal surface provides a higher corrosion-resistant ability. Therefore, CDs as aqueous phase corrosion inhibitors benefits from the huge surface coverage area and a large abundance of the coordinated chemical moieties ascribed to their nanomeric nature. Many literatures have reported that the solutions with CDs can effectively reduce the corrosion rate of steel, copper and aluminium in aggressively acidic, saline, CO2-saturated saline and microbiological solutions [15,16,37–40].

In this review, we outline the state of the art of CDs as powerful ecofriendly corrosion inhibitors for copper and its alloy. According to the different resource and chemical structure of CDs, we simply group CDs as corrosion inhibitors into three categories: (1) Heteroatom coped-CDs as corrosion inhibitors, (2) Biomass-derived CDs as corrosion inhibitors and (3) CDs-based nanocomposite as corrosion inhibitors. Finally, the current challenges and limitations, as well as the potential outlooks for exploiting CDs as powerful ecofriendly corrosion inhibitors for copper and its alloy, are also discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Classification of CDs as corrosion inhibitors for copper and its alloy

ce:section.ce:para: The corrosion inhibition ability of a corrosion inhibitor to protect copper from corrosion is highly depended on the presence of O, N, S and P heteroatoms in the molecular structure of the corrosion inhibitor and the film growth behavior controlled by the interactions between the inhibitor and the copper's surface. The adsorption process of inhibitors involves two types of interactions [41]. The physical adsorption process occurs with electrostatic interaction between the corrosion inhibitor and the opposite charged metal surface. The chemical adsorption process is attributed to the chelating bonds between the lone pair electrons of heteroatoms (P, N, S, O, etc.) or the unsaturated covalent bonds in the corrosion inhibitor structure with metal's surface. Therefore, the doping process by putting in heteroatoms can greatly enlarge the number of the heteroatoms on the surface of the CDs and thus lead to an improved corrosion inhibition ability. Undoubtedly, doped-CDs can behave as superior corrosion inhibitors as compared to the undoped CDs.

In 2021, Zhang et al. synthesized NCDs with citric acid and branched polyethyleneimine as raw materials at 180 °C [43]. The as-synthesized NCDs had an evenly spherical structure with a size distribution of 10–20 nm. The FT-IR, Raman, XRD and XPS experiments confirmed that there were an abundant of functional groups with N elements on the NCDs surface. The Tafel result indicated that NCDs was a moderate cathodic-type inhibitor (Fig. 3 a). The electrochemical measurement for copper in H2SO4 solution suggested that the prepared NCDs could effectively protect copper in sulfuric medium, and the IE value was as high as 96.1% at 100 mg/L (Fig. 3b). The adsorption process of these NCDs on Cu surface also obeyed a Langmuir adsorption model, and the adsorption involved chemical adsorption and physical adsorption. The formation of the protective film gone through the following steps: (1) NCDs was diffused to the metal/solution interface and some NCDs were adsorbed on the copper surface through chemical and physical adsorption. (2) Parts of the NCDs aggregated to form larger nanoparticles due to the agglomeration effect. (3) The heteroatoms of NCDs chelated with the free copper ions when the dissolved copper escaped from the interface into the corrosive medium. (4) Each NCDs bridged to form a network on the metal surface due to the chelating effect between the multiple coordinative sites and the copper ions and thus block the metallic active sites from the corrosive medium (Fig. 3c).

Solvothermal synthesis of NCDs from amino acid as corrosion inhibitors for copper in sulfuric acid solution was reported by Zhang's group [44] (Fig. 4 a). TEM indicated that the particle size of NCDs was uniformly distributed around 10 nm (Fig. 4b). These NCDs had an abundant of hydrophilic groups, unsaturated bonds and heterocyclic rings, which facilitate to be strongly adsorbed on the metal surface to form a stable protective layer. The electrochemical performance revealed that NCDs was a mixed-type corrosion inhibitor and the IE value increased up to 98.5% with the increased concentration, suggesting an excellent anticorrosive property.

Based on the above analysis, the doped nitrogen atom can improve the internal electronic structure and bring new electronic distribution states for CDs responsible for a satisfactory anti-corrosion effect for the copper in the aggressive acids. Therefore, the content of the nitrogen element in the CDs played an important role in improving the corrosion resistance. Namely, increasing the N/C ratio could improve the corrosion resistance. Zhang et al. prepared a high-nitrogen-doped CDs (h-NCDs) through a simple solvothermal treatment with melamine as the nitrogen source [45]. The morphology of the h-NCDs showed a spherical structure with size of ∼7 nm. The N content of the h-NCDs was as high as 23.95%, larger than that reported in the previous literatures 42 and 44. The optimal IE value was up to 96.1% at 200 mg/L in 3 wt% NaCl solution. The formed protective film of h-NCDs on copper surface had a rough morphology with many nanoparticles (Fig. 5 a and b). The surface contact angle of the corroded copper increased from 88o to 134o after adding h-NCDs (Fig. 5c and d), suggesting a change from a hydrophilic surface to a hydrophobic surface. The hydrophobic surface could retard the diffusion of the corrosive ions to the copper substrate, and inhibit the corrosion of copper metal. In addition, the chelating between Cu2+ ions and h-NCDs were responsible for the compactness of the protective film.

Except for mostly used solvothermal method, electrochemical exfoliation method has also been used to prepare NCDs. Zhou and coworkers developed a new micro-zone electrochemical exfoliation method for large-scale production of NCDs from pre-baked carbon anode (PCA) in ammonium bicarbonate electrolyte [46]. A rough diameter distribution of 5–50 nm was observed from the SEM images. These NCDs contained an abundant of functional groups and the doping content of nitrogen atoms was close to 30.18 at%. The IE value of the self-assembled NCDs film on the copper was as high as 96.32% at 150 mg/L in 1.0 mol/L sulfuric acid solution. The NCDs self-assembled on the copper surface to form a ‘‘grid-like’’ and compact protective film of NCDs/CuxO responsible for the effective corrosion resistance. The adsorbed NCDs themselves not only had a strong tendency to self-aggregate but also were easily bridged by the free dissolved Cu2+ through the chelating effect. Therefore, the adsorbed NCDs were self-assembled to form a “grid-like’’ protective film on the copper surface. The formed compact protective film of NCDs/CuxO could effectively isolate the copper surface from the corrosive medium and thus provide effective corrosion resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N, S-co-doped CDs as corrosion inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with the nitrogen atom, sulfur atoms has a strong affinity with the copper due to the stable Cu–S chemical bonds [47]. It is inferred that the NCDs doped with S atoms would result in a firmly adhesive protective film owe to forming strong coordination bonds between the sulfur atom and copper surface. Zhang et al. synthesized a novel N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) from phenylenediamine and thiourea as precursors by a simple solvothermal method (Fig. 6 a) [48]. The as-synthesized N, S-CDs showed evenly spherical fabric with a size distribution between 10 and 20 nm. From the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p (Fig. 6c–f), C1s spectrum showed three peaks centered at 284.7 eV, 285.1 eV and 285.9 eV attributed to C–C, C–N/C–O and C=N, respectively (Fig. 6c). The N1s spectrum had two peaks located at 399.2 eV and 399.8 eV correspond to N–H and C–N bands, respectively (Fig. 6d). For S2p spectrum, the two peaks associated with S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 of the C=S and C–S were centered at 162.9 and 161.5 eV, respectively. The peak at 168.4 eV was ascribed to C–S(O)x-C (Fig. 6f). The results proved the N, S-CDs have been successfully prepared. The FT-IR and Raman spectra also indicated that the N, S-CDs had abundant N, O and S groups. The corrosion inhibition performance of the N, S-CDs for copper in sulfuric acid solution was evaluated. The Tafel results showed that N, S-CDs was a mixed-type inhibitor, and the IE value increased along with increasing N, S-CDs concentration (Fig. 6b). These N, S-CDs displayed excellent corrosion resistance for copper (IE ∼99.88%) at the concentration of 50 mg/L in H2SO4 solution.

In 2010, Guo et al. reported a new class of N, S-CDs using glucose as a carbon source through a facile one-pot hydrothermal method and the anticorrosion performance of these N, S-CDs were measured on copper in neutral 3.5 wt% NaCl solution [49]. The authors believed that the glucose and ascorbic acid as raw materials were firstly dehydrated to form glucose-based CDs as the core and then the doped reagent (e.g. 4-amino-3-hydrazine-5-mercapto-1,2,4-triazole) was then coupled with carboxyl groups of the glucose-based CDs by the amidation reaction to give a new N, S-CDs. TEM images showed these N, S-CDs inhibitor showed a spherical morphology with size of 5–7 nm. SVET was applied to visualize the localized corrosion of copper in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution in the absence and presence of N, S-CDs. The high positive potential difference was observed on the metallic copper surface and the corresponding current density was also detected, suggested a serious corrosion occurred on the copper surface (Fig. 7a1 -d1). In contrast, The potential difference and current on copper surface greatly reduced, and the detected current density was quite small when adding these N, S-CDs into the aggressive medium (Fig. 7a2-d2), indicating an effective corrosion inhibition performance. The IE showed a tendency of increasing firstly and then decreasing with increasing corrosion inhibitor's concentration. The optimal IE could reach 88% at 70 ppm for copper in neutral NaCl solution. The protective film from these N, S-CDs was found to follow the Langmuir isotherm adsorption. The interactions between these inhibitors and copper atoms involved both chemisorption and physisorption. The adhesion force between the adsorption film and copper surface was measured by atomic force microscope. The five points on the copper surface were selected to measure the surface adhesive force (Fig. 8 a). The adhesive force characteristic were recorded on the copper surface immersed in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution in the force-distance curves (Fig. 8b), and the measured average adhesion was about 0 nN. However, an obvious adhesive force was detected and its average value was up to 2.5 nN when these N, S-CDs were added into the above neutral aggressive medium. This result clearly confirmed that a protective film from corrosion inhibitors were formed on the copper surface. The corrosion morphology of copper after 24 h immersion without and with N,S-CDs inhibitor was shown in Fig. 8c and d. A large amount of corrosion products on copper surface were observed without N, S-CDs attributed to the successive attack of corrosive chloride ions (Fig. 8c). However, the number of the corrosion products greatly decresed and only a few scratches were found on the copper surface after adding the inhibitor, indicating that the corrosion process was effectively inhibited (Fig. 8d). The above results clealy confirmed the formation of N, S-CDs film and its excellent inhibition effect for copper, which are consistent with the consequences of electrochemical tests.

Recently, Liu et al. prepared a family of N, S-CDs with different N/S ratios by adjusting the feeding ratio of L-cysteine and ammonium citrate through a solvothermal method [50]. The structure and morphology of N, S-CDs were fully characterized. The FT-IR and XPS results indicated that S and N elements were successfully doped into CDs. The N, S-CDs showed good solubility in the aqueous solution with average particle size of about 3–6 nm. The corrosion inhibition performance of the N, S-CDs for copper metal in hydrochloric acid environment was studied. The IE highly depended on the concentration and the S/N ratio of the N, S-CDs. The IE value increased with increasing N, S-CDs concentration. The IE of N, S-CDs displayed a first increasing and then decreasing trend with the increasing of S/N ratio. When the weight ratio of L-cysteine/ammonium citrate was 0.5, the icorr value had a minimum of 11 μA/cm2 at 160 mg/L and the IE value could reach 94 %. The N–Cu and S–Cu bonds from XPS indicated a chelating effect between the copper atom and N, S-CDs with the heteroatoms as active sites. The corrosion inhibitor adsorption on the solution/copper interface was a physicochemical adsorption. Substantial parameters of the computational study encompass the optimized structure of CDs corrosion inhibitors, Energy of Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO), Energy of Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) and energy gap (ΔE) were calculated from DFT calculation. Generally, higher value of HOMO indicates the more convenience of CDs for donating electrons to protect metal/alloy. Lower LUMO relates to the ability of inhibitor molecules to accept electrons. Electron density of both HOMO and LUMO is distributed in active sites of the CDs molecules. Larger adsorption capacity and higher reactivity are obtained on the lower ΔE. From Fig. 9 , The deeper blue (electrophilic activity)/yellow regions (nucleophilic activity) from ESP map and the low energy gap (ΔE = 1.17 eV) calculated by DFT indicated that N, S-CDs were easily covalently bonded with copper atoms and hence resulted in dense protective layer to block the active sites of the copper surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion-co-doped CDs as corrosion inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: Some rare earth element including Ce, La, Pr etc. can form low-soluble metal oxide/hydroxide on the cathodic intermetallic compounds and showed noticeable inhibitory effect [51]. Therefore, the rare earth element with corrosion inhibitory effect has also been coped in CDs to improve their corrosion resistance. Ye et al. prepared a new class of Ce and N co-doped carbon dots (Ce, N-CDs) from cerium nitrate and ammonium citrate through hydrothermal synthesis [52]. XPS results showed the content of Ce elements increased from 0% to 15%, while the content of N and O elements reduced from 9.1% and 31.3% to 7.5% and 27.9%, respectively, suggesting Ce atoms successfully doped. The morphology of the Ce, N-CDs was spherical or quasi-spherical particles with 2∼5 nm and the size distribution was relatively uniform. The content of Ce greatly affected the corrosion inhibition performance of Ce, N-CDs for copper in 1 M HCl solution. Among all the tested samples, the Ce-10% sample showed the best corrosion inhibition and the IE value could reach 98% at 200 mg/L. The 3D topography and 2D morphology of the corroded copper surface showed the corrosion of the copper surface was first alleviated and then intensified with the increase of Ce content (Fig. 10 a and b). The Ra value was 0.274, 0.270, 0.201, 0.230 μm when the Ce content was 0%, 5%, 10%, and 15%, respectively. The Ce-10% sample showed the highest corrosion inhibition, and the Ra of the electrode surface was reduced by 54.8% compared with the control sample in this case (Fig. 10a). A large amount of N and Ce elements were evenly distributed on the surface of electrode, indicating a dense adsorption/passivation film was formed on the surface (Fig. 10b). The adsorption process of the as-prepared Ce, N-CDs was controlled by physical and chemical processes responsible for the effective adsorption of Ce, N-CDs on the metal surface, which further blocking the copper surface from the corrosive media. Corrosion product analysis indicated the metal surface was covered by Ce, N-CDs adsorption film, Ce containing oxide and hydroxide passivation film, thereby delaying the corrosion process. The corrosion of the copper metal was highly suppressed by the combined action of adsorption film and passivation film.

The traditional synthesis of CDs involves the use of organic chemical reagents as reaction precursors, along with expensive experimental instruments. However, the conventional synthetic method is inconsistent with the principles of economic viability and environmental sustainability. Recently, using biomass and plastic wastes as carbon sources to prepare CDs has attracted intensive research interests because these eco-friendly approaches could not only reduce reliance on costly reagents and experimental instruments but also make contributions to the efficient recycling of waste materials. Biomass wastes including green tea, durian shells, coconut, mango pulp, custard apple peel and other biomass waste materials have been reported as raw materials to prepare CDs [53,54].

In 2018, Faradisa Anindita et al. prepared blue emitting biomass-derived CDs (BCDs) from durian juice and further studied the corrosion resistance for copper in the marine environment [55]. The as-prepared BCDs were characterized by FT-IR, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. A large abundant of electron rich polar functional groups such as C=O, O–H, and S=O were found on the surface of these BCDs, which could solubilize CDs in aqueous phase and act as adsorption active sites. Electrochemical and weight loss techniques were used to evaluate the corrosion resistance of these BCDs for the copper metal in 1 wt% NaCl solution. The IE value of these BCDs increased with the increasing of BCDs concentration and the highest IE value reached 86% at 800 ppm. PDP results indicated that BCDs could retard both anodic and cathodic reactions and make themselves as mixed-type inhibitors.

Subsequently, another blue fluorescence coffee carbon dots (CCDs) from green bean coffee powder and urea as raw materials were reported by Kalema Christopher et al. through a simple and green tandem hydrothermal and pyrolysis method [56]. The structure of this CCDs was studied by FT-IR, UV–Vis, and ocean optics spectrophotometer. The presence of hydrophilic groups including –OH, –NH2, C=O, and C–N was responsible for the excellent water solubility. The corrosion rate of the copper decreased with decreasing concentration of the CCDs, implying that CCDs had a corrosion inhibition activity. The highest IE value was up to 77% in 1 wt% sodium chloride solution. In addition, the as-synthesized CCDs exhibited quenching effect towards Cu2+ and Pb2+ ions and thus could be also used for Cu2+ and Pb2+ ions detection.

Recently, Li et al. designed and prepared a novel biomass-derived CDs (BCDs) from the mango pulp as raw materials via a hydrothermal method (Fig. 11 e) [57]. This synthetic method didn't result in a large amount of waste solvents and toxic byproducts and thus was considered as a green synthesis method. The as-synthesized BCDs had a uniform spherical size distribution of ∼10 nm (Fig. 11d). The structure of these BCDs was investigated by FT-IR, UV–Vis, XRD and XPS spectrum (Fig. 11a–c). Structural characterizations indicated that these BCDs contained a significant number of heteroatoms (such as N and S) and heteroatom-containing functional groups.

The anticorrosive property of these BCDs for copper metal in H2SO4 medium was measured by electrochemical experiments. The electrochemical results showed an impedance value of 6314.1 Ω cm2 in a 50 mg/L BCD solution at 298 K, significantly higher than that observed in the blank solution. The highest corrosion inhibition efficiency was as close as 92.97%. The superior corrosion resistance performance revealed that BCDs could effectively anchor to and adsorb on the copper surface. Electrochemical tests were conducted at different temperature to investigate the stability of the formed BCDs film (Fig. 12 a–f). The IE value still exceeded 90% even at 328 K, indicating that BCDs had good anticorrosive ability even at high temperature. In addition, some key thermodynamically related parameters were obtained from the electrochemical data (Fig. 12g and h). From the result, the copper has a lower activation energy (Ea) in a sulfuric acid medium, suggesting that Cu2+ was easily formed and diffused into the aggressive medium which resulted in a higher corrosion rate. However, after adding BCDs, the corrosion inhibitor could be adsorbed on the copper surface due to the chelating effect between the BCDs and Cu+ and thus produced a compact protective film which prevented the further diffusion of the copper ions. Therefore, the formation of the barrier retarded the corrosion rate of the copper. The above process required additional energy and thus resulted in an increase in the Ea. The positive value of activation enthalpy (ΔHa) revealed that the copper dissolution process was an endothermic process. A higher activation energy in the presence of BCDs implied the copper dissolution process was slower and more difficult than that in the absence of BCDs. To give a deepened exploration of the film-forming mechanism of BCDs on the copper surface, the morphology and structure of the corroded copper was further analyzed. Morphological characterizations revealed that BCDs formed barrier film through adsorption and coordination film mechanisms. BCDs were physically adsorbed on the metal surface and chemically chelating with Cu+ to form a compact and ordered protective film, which slowing the reaction rates of cathode and anode in the sulfuric acid and thus retarding copper corrosion. In addition, the fluorescence property of BCDs as a probe to further study the adsorption process. The fluorescence signal gradually weakened over time in the sulfuric acid containing BCDs, which further proved the physicochemical reaction between the BCDs and the copper. A summary of anticorrosive effect of various CDs for copper was represented in Table 1 .

The nano-size structure and abundant of functionalities enable a simple and homogeneous dispersion of CDs in polymer matrix. Especially, the heteroatom-coped-CDs with hydroxy, carboxyl, amide, thiol groups have a strong affinity with polymers through hydrogen bonding, van der Waals force and other weak interactions. The high mobility of the CDs in the polymer network endows the polymer–CD composite coatings with interesting self-healing property, which is an intriguing pursuit for the anticorrosive materials. Zhe et al. reported CDs as nanofillers could induce healing/self-healing and anti-corrosion properties in the polymer coating [58]. Ramezanzadeh et al. prepared a new polyaniline tailored graphene oxide quantum dot (GO-PAni) and the corrosion protection performance of epoxy coating with GO-PAni was remarkably improved [59]. CDs can also be used as intercalators to exfoliate and stabilize some 2D layered nanomaterials such as graphene and boron nitride to form CDs-based nanocomposites. These CDs-based nanocomposite was further blended with polymer matrix to develop composite coatings with intriguing anticorrosive property and self-healing ability for metal protection. Ye et al. used CDs intercalated graphene as nanofiller to improve the self-healing and corrosion resistance of waterborne epoxy coating [60].

CDs-based corrosion inhibitors are potential substances for preventing the corrosion process for copper and its alloy in various aggressive media. Heteroatom doped-CDs showed superior corrosion resistance due to forming a compact and thick protective film on the copper surface through a physical and/or chemical adsorption processes via the interactions between the lone pair electrons of heteroatoms (P, N, S, O, etc.) and the metal surface. In this review, heteroatom coped-CDs, biomass-derived CDs and CDs-based nanocomposite as corrosion inhibitors for copper and its alloy are summarized. The as-synthesized CDs mostly showed a spherical morphology with nano size and has good solubility in aqueous aggressive medium. The electrochemical results indicated that the CDs could effectively reduce the cathodic and/or anodic electrochemical reactions and thus resulted in a high inhibition efficiency, mostly up to 90%. The formed CDs-adsorption film on copper was responsible for the superior inhibition performance and the adsorption process mostly obeyed the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model. The adsorption process were as generally followed two steps: Firstly, the CDs was diffused to the metal/solution interface and adsorbed on the copper surface by chemical and physical adsorptions. Then the heteroatoms of CDs were chelated with the copper ions escaped from the copper interface into the corrosive medium. The multiple chelating sites of CDs were connected to form a network on the copper surface to isolate the copper from the corrosive medium, resulting an enhanced corrosion resistance.

In spite of great development of CDs as powerful ecofriendly corrosion inhibitors, many challenges still remain. Firstly, although the doping method can introduce various heteroatoms in the CDs, the precise chemical structure of the surface functionalities is still unclear. More efforts should be devoted to developing new strategies to produce functional CDs with controllable and tunable surface functionalities. Secondly, in-depth insight into the adsorption process and interactions between the metal and CDs at the molecular and nano-level is required for the deeper understanding of corrosion resistance mechanisms of CDs. Thirdly, the actual condition of the flowing fluids on the pipeline is dynamic and even multiphase flow. The inhibition performance in the actual flowing fluids has not been tested. The durability of the adsorbed inhibitors on the metal surface in the actual condition needs to be further explored. With rapid developments and increasingly exquisite research in chemistry, material sciences and nano science, we firmly believe that the research area of CDs as powerful ecofriendly corrosion inhibitors will continue to blossom and expand.

This research has been encouraged and supported by Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (202303021211329), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2021J01847), the Project of Subsidy Funds for Marine Economic Development in Fujian Province (FJHJF-L-2022-20) and the Open Fund of Fujian Province Key Laboratory of Ship and Ocean Engineering. Dr. Zhao would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22103092) for the financial supporting.

Jianbin Liao: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Qingkai Chu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology. Songmei Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhixiong Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Xianhui Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Dr. Qiang, Yu-Jie for fruitful discussions and editorial assistance.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, C.; Fu, Y.; Liu, C.; Liu, Y.; Hu, L.; Liu, J.; Bello, I.; Li, H.; Liu, N.; Guo, S.; Huang, H.; Lifshitz, Y.; Lee, S.-T.; Kang, Z., Carbon Dots as Fillers Inducing Healing/Self-Healing and Anticorrosion Properties in Polymers. Adv Mater 2017, 29 (32), 1701399

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and characterization of polyaniline tailored graphene oxide quantum dot as an advance and highly crystalline carbon-based luminescent nanomaterial for fabrication of an effective anti-corrosion epoxy system on mild steel
J Taiwan Inst Chem E

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ramezanzadeh, B.; Karimi, B.; Ramezanzadeh, M.; Rostami, M., Synthesis and characterization of polyaniline tailored graphene oxide quantum dot as an advance and highly crystalline carbon-based luminescent nanomaterial for fabrication of an effective anti-corrosion epoxy system on mild steel. J Taiwan Inst Chem E 2018, 95, 369-382

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on self-healing and corrosion resistance behaviors of functionalized carbon dot-intercalated graphene-based waterborne epoxy coating
J. Mater. Sci. Technol.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functionalization of h-BN by the exfoliation and modification of carbon dots for enhancing corrosion resistance of waterborne epoxy coating
Prog. Org. Coating
